topic: Unified angular coordinate θ = −lonE — V13 coordinate identity
our_argument_summary: Our review argues that θ = −lonE follows from the definition of geographic longitude (not dome physics), that fixing V12's broken coordinate proxy is maintenance (not prediction), that WGS84 dependency persists (OPEN-001), and that accuracy (7.3%) is 700× worse than standard geodesy. Verdict: Misleading.

relevant_files: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt — V12 coordinate system: theta = delta_lon × 0.9941, city pair tables
raw-text/05-model.txt — V12 formulas: theta = delta_lon × 0.9941, distance formula
raw-text/07-ai-context.txt — V12/V13 task list, OPEN-003 (EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-058 is a coordinate identity claim, not a monitored domain. The dome's V12 system uses theta = delta_lon × 0.9941 with law-of-cosines distance. V13 proposes θ = −lonE (removing the 0.9941 scaling). No code in the dome repository monitors or validates this identity. The coordinates page shows static city pair calculations with pre-computed theta values and errors.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: All coordinate computations are static. The 0.9941 factor is hardcoded. The city pair distances and errors are pre-computed and displayed as HTML. No live computation validates the coordinate system against real-time survey data.
data_sources_used: WGS84 geodetic distances between city pairs (comparison benchmark)
Polaris elevation angles (input to r(city) computation)
Solar noon UTC times (legacy V9 input, still shown in NH table)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: No error tolerance specified for the coordinate system. The 0.9941 factor has no theoretical derivation (listed as OPEN-003). If distances don't match, the dome can adjust the scaling factor or invoke aetheric refraction without constraint.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: No material code discrepancies. Our review correctly identifies that no monitoring exists and that the 0.9941 factor persists in distance_analysis.py. However, we cannot verify the distance_analysis.py claim since the file is not in our raw-text archive (it was referenced in the dome's repository but not extracted).

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Searched raw-text/04-coordinates.txt, 05-model.txt, and 07-ai-context.txt. No live monitoring domain for coordinate system validation. No API calls, no automated comparison. The V12/V13 task list in the AI context page describes coordinate work as in-progress development, not monitored prediction.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The identity θ = −lonE is the standard definition of geographic longitude measured westward from Greenwich. This is how polar stereographic and azimuthal equidistant projections work — the dome is just adopting the existing convention. Standard geodesy already has well-defined coordinate systems (WGS84, UTM) that handle this. The dome renames it as a 'unified angular coordinate' discovery.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Geographic longitude has been defined since the adoption of the Greenwich meridian in 1884 (International Meridian Conference). The relationship between westward angle and east longitude negation is a mathematical identity known for centuries. The dome adopted this identity after discovering that its V12 solar-noon-UTC proxy produced 183° errors in the Southern Hemisphere. This is a post-hoc recognition of an existing convention, not a prospective prediction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: CORRECTED FROM CYCLE 1 (was: true). The identity θ = −lonE does NOT derive from dome geometry. Our own detail_evidence says: 'The identity θ = −lonE is not derived from dome geometry. It is derived from the definition of longitude.' No dome-specific parameters (disc radius, firmament height, aetheric wave speed) enter the derivation. The Cycle 1 tag of 'true' was set when reviewing the 0.9941 factor (which has some dome geometry connection), but the current WIN-058 text is specifically about θ = −lonE, which is a pure coordinate convention. The tag must match the claim being reviewed.

holes_found.description: code_analysis tag mismatch: derives_from_dome is 'true' in wins.json but our own detail_evidence explicitly states 'The identity θ = −lonE is not derived from dome geometry.' These are contradictory. The tag was likely carried over from the Cycle 1 review, which evaluated the 0.9941 factor (arguably dome-derived via fitting). The current text evaluates θ = −lonE (not dome-derived — it's the definition of longitude). The tag must be corrected to false.
holes_found.recommendation: Change code_analysis.derives_from_dome to false in wins.json for WIN-058. Our own argument depends on this claim — if we tag it as dome-derived while arguing it isn't, a dome defender can point to our own metadata as contradicting our text.

holes_found.description: V12 vs V9 attribution confusion. Our detail_evidence says: 'The V12 formula used solar noon UTC as a proxy for θ, producing catastrophic errors in the Southern Hemisphere (Sydney: +32.5°...).' But the dome's coordinates page header says V12 uses 'theta = delta_lon × 0.9941' (NOT solar noon UTC). The solar noon UTC formula ('theta = (solar_noon_utc - 12.0) × 15 deg') appears under the V9 REFERENCE section. The city table for Southern Hemisphere cities appears to still use V9-era computations (listing 'Noon Alt' rather than Polaris elevation), but attributing the solar-noon proxy to V12 is at least imprecise. V12 updated the Northern Hemisphere formula but may have left the Southern Hemisphere using V9 calculations — this nuance is not explained in our text. A dome defender could object: 'V12 already replaced the solar noon proxy with delta_lon × 0.9941; you're confusing V9 with V12.'
holes_found.recommendation: Clarify the version attribution. The solar noon UTC proxy was V9 (and earlier). V12 introduced theta = delta_lon × 0.9941 for Northern Hemisphere but the Southern Hemisphere city table still shows V9-era theta values (e.g., Sydney theta=+32.5°, which matches the solar noon formula, not the V12 delta_lon formula). Explicitly note that V12 appears to have been an incomplete transition — the formulas were updated but the Southern Hemisphere implementation lagged. V13's θ = −lonE completed the unification. This more precise narrative is harder for a defender to attack.

holes_found.description: The 0.9941 factor's relationship to θ = −lonE is under-explained. Our text mentions that distance_analysis.py still applies 'delta_t = np.radians(t2 - t1) * 0.9941' — implying the V12 scaling persists alongside V13's θ = −lonE identity. But θ = −lonE means the angular coordinate is EXACTLY the negation of east longitude (scaling factor = 1.000), while 0.9941 implies a different scaling. These can't both be right simultaneously. Either V13 eliminated the 0.9941 factor (in which case citing distance_analysis.py is misleading if it still has old V12 code), or V13 kept the 0.9941 factor for distance calculations while using θ = −lonE for angular identity (in which case the 'unified' claim is weaker because two different angular scalings coexist). Our review doesn't resolve this tension.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence clarifying: either (a) the 0.9941 factor was dropped in V13 and distance_analysis.py is stale V12 code, or (b) V13 uses θ = −lonE for coordinate identity but still applies 0.9941 for distance calculations, which means two incompatible angular definitions coexist — strengthening the 'not unified' argument.

holes_found.description: The '700× worse than standard geodesy' claim in the finding needs verification. Our text says 7.3% mean error vs sub-0.01% for Vincenty/WGS84. 7.3 / 0.01 = 730, so '700×' is roughly correct. But 'sub-0.01%' for Vincenty is an understatement — Vincenty achieves sub-millimeter accuracy over thousands of kilometers, which is better than 0.001%. The actual ratio could be 7,000× or more depending on the baseline used. The '700×' figure is conservative but defensible. However, the finding states '600–700×' in the detail_evidence and '700×' in the finding line — minor inconsistency.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one consistent figure. Either '700×' (using 0.01% as baseline) or note that the actual ratio depends on the precision benchmark. The inconsistency (600–700× vs 700×) is minor but gives a defender a nit to pick.

holes_found.description: The Singapore OPEN-015 citation at the end may be incorrect. Our text says: 'the dome's own OPEN-015 concedes Singapore (r = 23,556 km) falls beyond the equatorial ring at r_eq = 14,105 km.' This is a strong argument showing the coordinate system breaks for equatorial cities. However, we don't verify this OPEN-015 reference against the dome's actual open problems list. If OPEN-015 doesn't say exactly this, or has been resolved, the citation is vulnerable.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify OPEN-015 against the dome's current open problems list (if available in raw-text). If unverifiable, qualify with 'as of V51.0' to lock the reference to a specific version.

holes_found.description: The Cycle 1 review raised a valid philosophical point that our current text still partially inherits: we argue that θ = −lonE 'is not derived from dome physics' (true) and that it follows from 'the definition of geographic longitude' (true). But we should acknowledge the counter-argument more explicitly: the dome defender might say 'we're not claiming θ = −lonE is a NEW discovery — we're claiming that recognizing it UNIFIES our coordinate system across hemispheres, which is a model improvement.' Our text does address this ('correcting a self-inflicted bug is maintenance, not prediction'), but the counter is buried. A dome defender reading the summary table would only see 'follows from the definition of geographic longitude' and might dismiss our review as not understanding their actual claim.
holes_found.recommendation: The finding line is already good. The detail text handles this well with the 'kernel of truth' acknowledgment. Minor issue — no change needed, but consider whether the summary finding could be slightly more precise about WHAT makes it misleading (it's the framing as a prediction, not the identity itself).

stronger_arguments.description: Internal version inconsistency: V13's θ = −lonE (scale factor = 1.000) contradicts the still-active V12 scaling factor of 0.9941. If θ truly equals −lonE, then theta should NOT be multiplied by 0.9941 in distance calculations. But distance_analysis.py still applies this factor. The dome's coordinate system contains two incompatible angular definitions: the V13 identity (θ = −lonE, scale = 1.000) and the V12 distance formula (delta_theta = delta_lon × 0.9941, scale = 0.9941). This 0.6% angular discrepancy propagates to distance errors. Rather than 'unifying,' V13 introduced a second angular convention without retiring the first. This is not unification — it's layering.
sources: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt: 'theta = delta_lon x 0.9941' (V12 formula, still shown as current)
WIN-058 detail_evidence: references distance_analysis.py still applying 0.9941

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's 'prediction' is operationally equivalent to discovering that their AE map is centered on the North Pole — which is a DESIGN CHOICE they made, not a discovery about nature. On any north-pole-centered azimuthal equidistant projection, the angular coordinate is θ = −lonE by construction. The dome chose to use an AE projection, then 'discovered' that the angular coordinate on an AE projection behaves like the angular coordinate on an AE projection. This is circular: the coordinate convention was built into the map from the start.
sources: Snyder, J.P. (1987). Map Projections: A Working Manual. USGS Professional Paper 1395. — Standard reference on azimuthal equidistant projection properties.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome team genuinely improved their coordinate system. V9/V12 used solar noon UTC as an indirect proxy for angular position, which catastrophically broke for Southern Hemisphere cities (Sydney: 183° error). Recognizing that θ = −lonE directly provides the correct angle, without proxy indirection, is legitimate engineering improvement. The V13 fix eliminates an entire class of errors. The dome is also transparent about this being a fix: they describe the V12→V13 transition and acknowledge the V12 errors. This is how model development should work — identify bugs, fix them, document the improvement.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Fixing a self-inflicted coordinate bug is model maintenance, not a 'confirmed prediction.' The identity θ = −lonE was available since the 1884 International Meridian Conference and is trivially true on any north-pole-centered projection. The dome didn't predict this relationship — they belatedly recognized they should have been using geographic longitude directly instead of an error-prone proxy. No dome-specific physics enters the derivation. Calling a bug fix a 'WIN' inflates the confirmed prediction count, which is precisely the kind of misleading framing our review identifies.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 Comparison with Cycle 1:

The Cycle 1 review found critical/major issues and recommended a major_rewrite. The current text appears to be that rewrite — it is substantially different from what Cycle 1 described. The old argument ('coordinate choice is convention, not a physical prediction') has been replaced with a more targeted argument: (1) θ = −lonE follows from longitude definition, (2) fixing a self-inflicted V12 bug is maintenance, (3) WGS84 dependency persists, (4) accuracy is 700× worse than geodesy.

This is a significant improvement. The philosophical weakness flagged in C1 (coordinates are arbitrary → proves too much) has been addressed by grounding the argument in specific testable consequences. The 183° Sydney error is concrete and verifiable. The WGS84 dependency argument (OPEN-001) directly challenges the dome's claim of independence from globe-derived data.

Remaining issues are:
1. The code_analysis tag derives_from_dome=true contradicts the text (should be false)
2. V12 vs V9 attribution could be more precise
3. The 0.9941 factor's coexistence with θ = −lonE creates an internal contradiction in the dome's system that we should exploit more directly
4. The AE projection circularity argument (discovering that an AE projection has AE projection properties) is not yet in our text

The verdict 'Misleading' now seems well-supported: the dome frames a bug fix + adoption of existing convention as a 'confirmed prediction,' which is the definition of misleading framing. The Cycle 1 suggestion to change to 'Not Demonstrated' no longer applies because the rewritten text makes a clear case for why it's misleading specifically.

Overall assessment: Much improved since Cycle 1. Two actionable fixes (tag correction, version attribution), plus one enhancement opportunity (0.9941 coexistence argument).

citations_verified: The reference to Vincenty inverse formula and WGS84 ellipsoid for sub-0.01% accuracy is standard geodetic knowledge. Vincenty (1975) 'Direct and Inverse Solutions of Geodesics on the Ellipsoid with Application of Nested Equations' is the canonical reference. The precision claim is well-established.
The International Meridian Conference of 1884 established the Greenwich meridian — this is historical fact.
OPEN-001, OPEN-003, OPEN-015 are referenced as dome-internal open problems. OPEN-001 (WGS84 dependency) and OPEN-003 (0.9941 physical interpretation) are confirmed in raw-text/07-ai-context.txt.
citations_failed: The reference to distance_analysis.py applying 'delta_t = np.radians(t2 - t1) * 0.9941' cannot be verified — this file is not in our raw-text archive. We reference code we cannot confirm. If the dome has updated this file since we last extracted it, the claim could be stale.
citations_unchecked: OPEN-015 (Singapore beyond equatorial ring) — not found in our raw-text extracts. May be from V51.0 content not yet archived.

The specific V12→V13 transition narrative (when V13 was implemented, what exactly changed) comes from the dome's wins page for V51.0. Since WIN-058 is new_in_v51, the raw text from V50.6 doesn't include it. The V51 text that describes this WIN is not in our archive.
